metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = quantum reality (wave, curvature, zero-point energy); CRIMSON = classical expectation (particle at rest, zero energy floor)
metadata.note: Exclusions: no full boundary-value derivation, no normalization constant, no n-squared spectrum table, no uncertainty-principle algebra beyond one plain sentence
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

source_of_truth: beat_sheet.json — edit the sheet, not this file

beats.label: This is Liam, in for Bear. A particle trapped in a box cannot be brought to rest. Cool it to absolut

beats.prompt: Draw the energy levels for a particle in a box. The rungs climb upward — and the lowest one is not at the floor. It floats above zero. That gap is the zero-poin
beats.label: Draw the energy levels for a particle in a box. The rungs climb upward — and the lowest one is not a

beats.prompt: Classical reasoning: a particle at rest has zero kinetic energy. Make the box smaller, slow it down more, bring it to a complete stop. Energy should be zero.
beats.label: Classical reasoning: a particle at rest has zero kinetic energy. Make the box smaller, slow it down

beats.prompt: Classical picture: shrink the box, slow the particle, remove energy until it stops. Zero energy is reachable. The floor is the floor.
beats.label: Classical picture: shrink the box, slow the particle, remove energy until it stops. Zero energy is r

beats.prompt: But a trapped particle is a wave. The wavefunction must go to zero at both walls — that is the boundary condition. A wave that hits zero at both ends must fit a
beats.label: But a trapped particle is a wave. The wavefunction must go to zero at both walls — that is the bound

beats.prompt: A flat wave has zero curvature and zero kinetic energy. But a wave that curves — that bends to hit zero at both walls — has curvature. The Schrodinger equation
beats.label: A flat wave has zero curvature and zero kinetic energy. But a wave that curves — that bends to hit z

beats.prompt: The ground state fits exactly one half-wavelength. Shrink the box and that half-wave must squeeze tighter — it curves more, so its kinetic energy rises. The ene
beats.label: The ground state fits exactly one half-wavelength. Shrink the box and that half-wave must squeeze ti

beats.prompt: This is not a quirk of boxes. Every confined quantum system has a positive energy floor — a zero-point energy. Liquid helium refuses to freeze at absolute zero
beats.label: This is not a quirk of boxes. Every confined quantum system has a positive energy floor — a zero-poi

beats.prompt: You cannot remove the zero-point energy by cooling. It is irreducible — not thermal, but structural. The confinement itself is the source.
beats.label: You cannot remove the zero-point energy by cooling. It is irreducible — not thermal, but structural.

beats.prompt: Box width 0.5 nanometers — roughly an atomic diameter. Ground state energy E-one equals pi-squared h-bar-squared over 2 m L-squared. Plug in the numbers: E-one
beats.label: Box width 0.5 nanometers — roughly an atomic diameter. Ground state energy E-one equals pi-squared h

beats.prompt: A particle in a box cannot sit still because confinement forces the wavefunction to curve, and curvature is kinetic energy. The ground state is always above zer
beats.label: A particle in a box cannot sit still because confinement forces the wavefunction to curve, and curva
Your turn. Ask Claude: show me how the ground-state energy changes when I halve the box width, and e
Why a Particle in a Box Cannot Sit Still. Liam, in for Bear.
